title: Julie Buring, ScD, Former Research Director, Osher 20th ...
text:
thank you thank you good morning
everyone um i'm very honored to be here
today to be celebrating the 20th
anniversary of the osher center
um what i want to do is i'm going to be
talking
about my thoughts
about the importance and evolution of
research at the oceans at usher center
and as i was thinking about this talk
i was remembering with such nostalgia my
years as the first um director research
director of the osher center while also
realizing how much the research of osha
has grown and evolved so appropriately
and so successfully over time
so i would like to touch briefly on
three things number one how i got here
what attracted me to the osher center
number two how our research questions
have changed so the progression of
research from does it work to how does
it work
and finally what's next the current and
future role of research at the osher
center
so
how did i get here
my collaboration with the osher center
started about 20 years ago
when i first got to know david eisenberg
and had the opportunity to work with him
on research philosophy and research
strategies of the center
in my usual research job besides getting
the opportunity to work at the osha
center i was and am doing very
traditional research i'm conducting
large-scale randomized trials looking at
whether there are simple things
that we can do to reduce our risk of
developing chronic diseases like heart
disease and cancer for the very first
time
so specifically i evaluate the possible
preventive effects of a number of
promising but unproven hypotheses
such as low dose aspirin
nutritional supplements such as vitamin
d vitamin e vitamin c fish oil and
multivitamins
and nutritional changes such as reducing
salt or reducing fats
and for every trial i do
the overarching goal is the same
to provide solid evidence that will
advance our knowledge and thus inform
evidence-based recommendations
similarly the core scientific
underpinning of the trials are the same
to conduct a study whose results are
judged by our scientific community
to be valid or true
to disseminate the knowledge widely
through publications and presentations
and to inform regulatory agencies such
as the fda or professional organizations
such as the american cancer society
so they can consider these data in
making their clinical and their public
health recommendations
but meanwhile i was becoming more and
more fascinated with the field of what
then was called cam complementary and
alternative medicine i was fascinated by
its approaches fascinated by its
extensive history
but when it came to the evidence-based
data that i wanted to see
no differently then i want to see the
evidence-based data if i'm deciding to
take a newly developed drug
or even if i'm trying to figure out to
get a second booster shot for covid
and those evidence-based data for cam
therapies were rarely there
and making it even more complex there
also seemed to be a tremendous amount of
emotion
associated with the use or non-use of
cam
there were groups that firmly believed
that decades
centuries of use provided sufficient
evidence of efficacy
and others who would remain firmly
skeptical or even dismissive regardless
of the evidence
even the names used
both don mentioned it peter mentioned it
so
complementary alternative
unconventional non-traditional
unorthodox
suggested that these therapies were
considered promising but unproven by
traditional practitioners
so i became increasingly convinced that
the goal of all research and into in
integrative therapies
has to provide the same high standards
of evaluation that are expected and
required of conventional medicine with
its western methods
but i was also more than a little naive
when i first got started doing research
in this field i had no idea just how
complex evaluating integrative therapies
with the highest level of rigor and
precision would be
i was used to designing a trial looking
at something like if we take omega-3
fatty acids fish oil supplements for the
prevention of cardiovascular disease
would it be effective
and when i was designing the trial there
were already broadly accepted
definitions of the dose and formulation
of fish oil interventions and commonly
accepted cardiovascular disease
manifestations to use as outcomes
however when peter wayne for example
began his trials of tai chi for whole
body stability in women at risk of
fallen related fractures
it was not yet clear what would
constitute an appropriate dose or
regimen of tai chi
what would be objective and clinically
meaningful outcomes of balance that
should be used
and what were the mechanisms by which
tai chi would be working to increase
balance
so to me it became clear
that no one could question that doing a
valid study in integrative therapies can
be an on an order of magnitude more
challenging than more traditional
interventions
but the bottom line is if we're going to
change culture and we're going to have
impact
everything that's being explored or
newly claimed or outside the system
requires rigorous science with a goal of
translating the evidence
into a recommendation
for something as challenging to evaluate
as cam
there will be even more scrutiny and
higher bars of evidence for acceptance
of the findings
and evaluating it rigorously
will it require new teams
and new methods and new paradigms and
new models of thinking and the breaking
of new ground
and a fundamental commitment to
connections
when david eisenberg published his paper
indicating that a third of the u.s were
using cam
it was clear that many were using these
therapies without a definitive evidence
of benefit or harm
and though thus it also became clear
that it was urgent and imperative for us
to provide the evidence in ways that
would be widely accepted
if a therapy anything tai chi
acupuncture
is demonstrated through our accepted and
common research language to be
efficacious and safe with respect to a
particular outcome
then more people should be considering
using it
and if it's not demonstrated to be
effective or safe then people need to
know that too
in order to inform their own
decision-making process and allow our
precious funding resources to be turned
to exploring other promising paths
so how have our research
how have our research questions changed
the first stage of the research at the
osher center
focused on the clinically important and
timely questions of whether can
therapies work
is a given therapy efficacious and safe
for a particular outcome
but then in the next stages of our
research investigators such as helen and
peter
took the evaluation of questions to the
next level
by asking not just if a technique works
but how
what are the mechanisms
so elucidating mechanisms requires
cutting edge techniques like medical
imaging or biomechanics to move the
field forward
but it equally requires the novel
methodology and ways of thinking
developed by ted kapchuk and katherine
hall
to both better design and better
interpret the study findings
so one example
of the interdisciplinary technique
that are now used can be seen in a study
by peter wayne on which i was a
collaborator
in which he evaluated the impact of tai
chi
on fracture related risk factors
in post-menopausal women
and one of the most difficult components
of the design of this study was how do
you assess objective measures of balance
so peter approached the motion analysis
laboratory at the spaulding
rehabilitation hospital here in boston
and in a biomotion sub-study they used
video camera-based movement analysis
systems that included infrared cameras
and force
flap
platforms
this involved a lot of specialized work
and expense
but substantially maximized the
credibility of the study results
the first time i saw these data i can't
even tell you how excited i was this was
a truly interdisciplinary approach
connecting integrative therapies and
biomechanics in the language that could
be understood and respected by all
and then poor peter i invited him to
give a seminar on this study to every
one of my research design classes but
mainly so the students could see how
working together
the basics of our respective techniques
can apply to a wide range of important
non-traditional questions
and so what's next
looking forward rigorous research will
always be a major part of the osha
strategic plan
we will still evaluate the core
questions of whether an intervention
works using novel methods to evaluate
but we'll also increasingly evaluate it
using objective markers of mechanisms
in addition we will increasingly connect
with leading edge
technology and tools to address
important questions for our aging
society
from the bread and butter questions
related to diseases such as
cardiovascular disease chronic
obstructive lung disease and metabolic
syndrome
to the compelling common and practical
concerns
about the prevention of falls and
fractures the prevention of loss of
cognitive function and getting dementia
with aging
so evaluation of these mind-body
questions are being led by peter and
gloria yeah
are heavenly heavily interdisciplinary
and international in scope and include
leading edge technology and tools
for example there are two real gems of
grants that are currently under
consideration for funding from the
national institutes of health
the first one in collaboration with
hebrew senior life
we've proposed a randomized trial of tai
chi versus health education
in reducing the loss of cognitive
function with aging
but in an exploratory
sub-component
they're looking at
fmri brain imaging to characterize how
resting and task-based functional
connectivity connectivity
are impacted by the interventions
and how what you see on the imaging
relates to improvements in executive
health and gait health
and in the second trial measures of
daily living gait and daily living
physical activity will be evaluated with
wearable sensors and machine learning
tools for their association with risk of
injurious falls in order to identify
high risk older adults who may
particularly benefit from closer
clinical management and evidence-based
fall prevention strategies
so the bottom line is that i think the
basic concepts of rigorous scientific
evaluation won't change but the
questions they are going to be applied
to will change and the evidence that's
being generated by program centers like
the osher center will be what moves our
our field forward scientifically
but it will also foster the growth of
our future leaders who will continue our
mission thank you very much
[Applause]
so now we're going to hear from gloria
yay
who is
both the director of clinical research
as well as the director of the t32
postdoctoral fellowship in integrative
medicine and dr yay will be speaking on
moving forward in research
you
